summary: Leaked documents reveal Palantir's extensive client list including the CIA, NSA, FBI, LAPD, and military contractors. A quoted military contractor's claim that Palantir lets you 'know every single bad guy in your area' demonstrates a dangerous misunderstanding of data mining's limitations. O'Neil argues that no algorithm can be both comprehensive and accurate—there will always be false positives or false negatives. The lack of transparency around these powerful tools used by law enforcement and intelligence agencies makes this particularly concerning.
key_points: Palantir's clients as of 2013 included major government agencies (CIA, NSA, FBI, DHS, CDC) and the LAPD
Military contractors wrongly believe data mining tools can identify 'every single bad guy' with perfect accuracy
No data mining algorithm can simultaneously achieve comprehensiveness and accuracy—false positives and negatives are inevitable
Companies have financial incentives to oversell their tools as prophetic even when they regularly fail
The combination of powerful surveillance tools and zero public oversight is deeply troubling
money_quotes: No data mining tool can be both comprehensive and accurate – find all the bad guys with no accidental good guys getting caught in the net.

It behooves an expensive and fancy consulting company to act like their tools are prophetic, however, even if that means false positives or false negatives happen all the time, which of course they do, with any algorithm.
It's bad enough when stupid start-up companies claim big data solves everything, when what they're doing is trying to solve a problem nobody cares about.
It's another thing altogether when it's our military and military contractors and police and secret services, and when we don't have any view into what it actually does.
key_insight: The dangerous combination of oversold algorithmic capabilities and zero transparency becomes especially alarming when the clients are military, police, and intelligence agencies with life-and-death power.
